segments.s502-g57: This clause strings three past actions with the coordinating conjunction و. بخورد is the classical past (3rd person singular) of خوردن, here “he ate.” برآسود is a past/inchoative form meaning “was at rest/relieved,” and برگشت is the simple past “he returned.” زود is an adverb (“soon/quickly”) placed after برگشت to modify that verb. The و’s simply link the successive actions.
segments.s502-g58: ستایش is the noun “praise” (the subject). همی is the archaic/progressive particle used in epic Persian to mark continued or habitual action (here: “kept/continually”). بافرین functions as an adjectival/adverbial phrase meaning “with praise” or “praisefully.” بر is the preposition “upon/over,” and فزود is the past (3rd singular) of فزودن “to increase.” Together the word order places the noun and modifiers before the verb, yielding “praise continually, with acclaim, increased upon (him).”
segments.s502-g17: شب تیره کرد is a causative clause: شب (night) is the direct object of تیره کرد, where کرد is used in the sense 'to cause/make' (causative), so literally 'made the night dark'. از جهاندار یاد is a prepositional/causal phrase introduced by از meaning 'because of' or 'on account of' and links as the cause: 'because of the remembrance of the sovereign.'
segments.s502-g18: پس here means 'then' (sequence). اندیشه نهاد is a light-verb construction where نهاد is past tense of نهادن 'to place/put' and takes اندیشه as its object: 'he placed/laid his thought'. بر is the preposition 'upon/on', and آب حیوان is a poetic compound 'water of life' ('آب' used figuratively). The verb nهاد links the whole phrase: 'then he set his thought upon the water (of life).'
segments.s502-g19: شکیبا is an adjective 'patient' used as a predicate or descriptive nominative. ز لشگر is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from the army' (ز = از). هرانکس که دید is a free relative 'whoever he saw' (هرانکس = 'whoever; anyone', که دید = 'that he saw'), and it functions as the object or complement of the following verb.
segments.s502-g20: نخست is an adverb 'first/initially'. از میان سپه is the prepositional phrase 'from among the host' (میان = 'among/middle', سپه = 'army/host') and specifies the domain of choice. برگزید is the past tense verb 'chose/selected' that takes the preceding relative clause ('whoever he saw') as its object: 'he first chose whoever he saw from among the host.'
segments.s502-g21: چهل روزه افزون is a temporal phrase: چهل روزه 'forty-day (fast)' with افزون 'additional/more', meaning 'an added forty days' of fasting. خورش برگرفت uses برگرفت (past of برگرفتن) 'took/received/ate' with خورش 'a cooked dish/meal', so the clause means he resumed or took food after that period. The sequence implies he prolonged fasting and then took a meal.
segments.s502-g22: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. دمان (here 'دمان') means 'skirts/outskirts' or 'edge' and is the place he approached. تا introduces purpose 'so that/in order that' and governs the subjunctive-like report چه بیند 'what he might see' (بیند = present/subjunctive 'may see' or 'might see'), with شگفت 'marvel/wonder' as the object: 'he came up to the outskirts to see what marvel he would behold.'
segments.s502-g23: سپه را uses the object marker را (marks the definite object 'the army'). بران is a poetic locative 'upon that/thereon' (equivalent to 'بر آن' or 'بر این'), and شارستان means 'city/town'. جای کرد is the verb phrase 'placed/made a place', with کرد as the light verb 'made/did': 'he set the army in that town.'
segments.s502-g24: یکی 'a certain one/someone' begins the clause. پیش رو functions as a locative/adverbial 'to the front/forward' (پیش = 'in front', رو = directional 'toward'), and چست is the adjective 'nimble/quick'. بر پای کرد is an idiomatic verb phrase where بر + پای + کردن literally 'made (himself) on foot' meaning 'took his stand/advanced on foot' or 'sprang forward' — so: 'one nimble fellow went forward and took his stand.'
segments.s502-g49: همی رفت is a habitual/continuous past construction: همی (still/kept on) + رفت (went), so ‘kept going’. ازین سان is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in this way’ (از + این(=ین) + سان). دو روز و دو شب is a duration phrase (‘two days and two nights’) placed after the verb, following classical Persian word order.
segments.s502-g50: کسی را is the affected/topic noun with the object marker را; here, with the negative verb, it functions idiomatically as ‘no one’. به + خوردن is the preposition plus infinitive meaning ‘to/for eating’. نجنبید is the negative past form of جنبیدن (‘did not move’); لب (‘lip’) is the inner object of نجنبید (literally ‘did not stir a lip’ = ‘did not open a mouth to eat’). Word order puts the infinitive construction before the negative past verb.
segments.s502-g51: سه دیگر is a numeral + modifier meaning ‘three more/three others’. به تاریکی اندر دو راه is a locative phrase: به تاریکی ‘into/in the darkness’ and اندر (archaic) ‘in/within’ + دو راه ‘two-way/road’ (a fork). The locative phrase follows the numeral phrase, as typical in Persian.
segments.s502-g52: پدید آمد is a light-verb construction meaning ‘appeared’ (پدید + آمد). و گم شد is ‘and was lost/vanished’ where شد is the auxiliary creating the resultant state. از خضر شاه indicates source/point of separation (‘from/before Khizr, the king’), i.e. they disappeared away from Khizr’s presence or sight.
segments.s502-g53: پیمبر is the subject ‘the prophet’. سوی آب is a directional phrase ‘toward the water’ (سوی = toward). حیوان کشید is verb + object in the typical classical order where the object (حیوان) appears before the past verb کشید (here ‘drove/pulled/led’). The verb is past tense (کشید).
segments.s502-g54: سر زندگانی is a noun phrase ‘the head/chief of life’ (سر = head; زندگانی = life) functioning as the object. به کیوان کشید is ‘he drew/toward Kayvān’ (به = toward; کیوان = Kayvān/Saturn), with کشید again the past verb. This is a figurative motion clause: the object is moved toward a goal introduced by به.
segments.s502-g55: بران is an adverbial/deictic ‘thereupon/thereon’ linking to the previous action. آب روشن is a noun + adjective ‘clear/bright water’. سر و تن (head and body) is a coordinated object, followed by the past verb بشست (‘he washed’)—classical SOV ordering with compound object before the verb.
segments.s502-g56: نگهدار جز پاک یزدان نجست is structured as object phrase + verb: نگهدار ‘a guardian/protector’, جز پاک یزدان ‘except the Pure God’ (جز = except), and نجست is the past of جستن ‘sought’. The clause therefore means ‘(he) sought no guardian except the Pure God’ (subject omitted, as is common).
segments.s502-g1: وزان جایگه is an adverbial phrase: وزان (= 'from that') + جایگه ('place'). شاد modifies لشگر, forming a noun phrase 'شاد لشگر' = 'the joyful/cheerful host'. براند is a past third‑person verb ('he drove/led'); the verb follows the phrase and completes the clause: 'from that place he led the joyful army.'
segments.s502-g2: بزرگان بیدار دل را is the direct object marked by the object particle را. بیدار دل is a compound adjective (literally 'awake‑hearted' = 'alert‑hearted') modifying بزرگان ('the great ones/elders'). بخواند is a past third‑person verb here meaning 'he called/summoned' (an epic narrative use), so the whole clause means 'he summoned the great, alert‑hearted ones.'
segments.s502-g3: همی رفت is a habitual/progressive past construction: همی gives a continuous/iterative nuance ('kept going'). تا introduces the limit 'until', followed by سوی شهری رسید ('he reached/took him to a city') where سوی + noun means 'toward/to a city' and رسید is past 'arrived/reached.'
segments.s502-g4: که begins a relative clause modifying 'city'. آن را is the definite pronoun with the object marker را (here referring back to the city), and میان و کرانه are parts/locations ('middle and margin/border') of that city. ندید is past negative 'he did not see', so the clause says he could not see the city's center or its bounds.
segments.s502-g5: همه هرچ ('everything/whatever') is a general noun phrase. باید here has a stative/classical sense of 'was fitting / belonged' (not a modern modal 'must') and is followed by بدو ('to it' / 'for it'), so the phrase means 'everything appropriate to it' or 'all that it needed'. در فراخ is a locative phrase 'in the wide/spacious (place)'.
segments.s502-g6: پر از is a fixed expression 'full of'. It governs a list of nouns joined by و: باغ (garden), میدان (field/public square/arena), ایوان (iwan/porch) and کاخ (palace). The whole segment describes the spacious place as filled with those features.
segments.s502-g7: فرود آمد is a past verb phrase meaning 'he alighted/descended'. The coordinating و links the verb phrase to the time expression بامداد پگاه: both words mean 'dawn/early morning' and together form a temporal adverbial 'at early dawn'.
segments.s502-g8: به نزدیک + آن چشمه is a prepositional phrase 'to/near that spring' (به نزدیک = 'near/toward'). شد here indicates the change/state 'became/was' in a narrative sense ('came to be near' / 'approached'), and بی‌سپاه is an adverbial/phrase meaning 'without an army' or 'unescorted'. Together: 'he approached that spring unescorted.'
segments.s502-g41: ببینیم is a hortative/subjunctive first‑person plural 'let us see' (verb before clause, common in poetry). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that' / 'in order that'). کردگار جهان is a noun phrase with کردگار ('Creator/God') modified by جهان ('of the world') — literally 'Creator of the world'.
segments.s502-g42: بدین آشکارا is a prepositional/absolutive phrase: بدین (به + این 'by/with this') + آشکارا (adverb 'manifestly/clearly'). چه دارد نهان is a question/relative content clause: چه 'what', دارد (third‑person singular present) as main verb 'has/keeps', and نهان 'hidden' as the thing kept — together 'what (he) has kept hidden'.
segments.s502-g43: توی پیش رو‌گر functions as a locative/epithet group: توی 'in/among', پیش 'before/ahead', and رو‌گر is an agentive epithet ('one who goes forward' / 'vanguard‑type', an older compound formation found in poetry). پناه من اوست is a copular clause: پناه 'refuge', من 'my' (genitive), اوست 'he is'. Together: 'In the vanguard (or: among those who go before), he is my refuge.'
segments.s502-g44: نماینده رای و راه من اوست is another copular clause. نماینده 'representative/agent' is the predicate noun; رای و راه من ('my counsel and my way') is a genitive phrase modifying نماینده; اوست is the copula 'he is'. Word order (predicate before copula) is typical of Persian verse.
segments.s502-g45: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. لشگر is the subject 'the army/host'. سوی آب حیوان is a prepositional phrase 'toward the water of Hayvān' (آب + حیوان in a genitive relation); گذشت is the past tense verb 'passed' (third person). The clause means 'when the army passed toward the water...' (classical word order places the verb at the end).
segments.s502-g46: خروش آمد is 'a roar/cry came' (noun + past verb آمد, a common idiom for 'a sound arose'). الله‌اکبر is the shouted phrase rendered literally here; ز دشت is a prepositional phrase 'from the plain' (ز = from, دشت = plain). The whole: 'a cry "Allahu Akbar" came from the plain.'
segments.s502-g47: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. از منزلی 'from a dwelling/place' uses از + a noun in the oblique ('from a place'). خضر is the proper name Khizr (a figure often appearing in Persian verse). برداشتی is a past form 'took away / lifted / left' — in poetic diction the suffix shape may vary, but here it reports the past action 'left/took away' associated with خضر.
segments.s502-g48: خورشها 'the stews/dishes' (plural with the -ها suffix). ز هرگونه 'of every kind' is a partitive/genitive expression (ز = 'of/from' in poetic use, هرگونه = 'every sort'). بگذاشتی is a past/action verb 'left/put aside' (you/he left), here meaning 'he left behind' — together: 'he left the dishes of every kind behind.'
segments.s502-g25: ورا (vora) is the direct object pronoun 'him' placed before the locative phrase; اندر آن = 'in/within that'. خضر here is a poetic/classical word placed as the locative noun phrase ('that place/seat' — in some manuscripts a proper name, in others a descriptive word), and بد رای زن is an adjectival/participial cluster meaning 'one who gives ill or adverse counsel' (بد + رای 'bad counsel' with زن functioning like an agentive/participial element). The whole segment therefore reads 'he put him in/into that (place), (as) a giver of bad counsel' — in context the phrase marks the status/character assigned to the man placed there.
segments.s502-g26: This is a noun phrase in Persian head-first order: سر 'head/chief' followed by the group it governs. نامداران 'the renowned ones' modifies/identifies who the head is of, and آن انجمن 'that assembly' localizes the group. Implicit ezāfe is classical/poetic, so the literal structure is 'the head of the renowned (men) of that assembly.'
segments.s502-g27: سکندر بیامد is a simple past clause 'Alexander came.' به فرمان اوی is a prepositional phrase 'at his command' or 'by his order'; اوی is the archaic/poetic third‑person possessive/enclitic meaning 'his' (here showing whose command). Word order is standard classical Persian: verb after subject, then circumstantial phrase.
segments.s502-g28: دل و جان سپرده is a nominal phrase using the past participle سپرده 'entrusted' attributively: 'heart and life entrusted'. به پیمان اوی is a dative/benefactive phrase 'to his covenant' (اوی = 'his'). In classical Persian the participle + prepositional phrase expresses the state/result ('having entrusted heart and life to his covenant').
segments.s502-g29: بدو گفت is the light‑verb construction 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'); کای is the vocative 'O' (classical poetic address). مرد بیداردل is a compound epithet 'man of wakeful (or alert) heart' — an honorific address. Note that classical Persian often strings vocative + epithets before the imperative/command that follows.
segments.s502-g30: یکی تیز گردان is a noun phrase 'one swift troop/detachment' (یکی = 'one/a single', تیز = 'swift/keen', گردان = 'troop/detachment'). بدین کار is the prepositional/demonstrative phrase 'for this work' (بدین = 'to/for this' + کار 'task'). دل at the end functions as a vocative or emphatic address 'O heart' (an affectionate or exhortative form). The imperative force is implied in the verb-less command structure typical of epic style.
segments.s502-g31: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). آب حیوان is an idiomatic/genitive phrase 'the living water' or 'water of life' (آب in a figurative sense of vital water), and به چنگ آوریم is a verbal phrase 'we seize/capture' literally 'bring to claw' (به چنگ آوردن = 'to get into one's grasp'). آوریم is first‑person plural present/subjunctive used for conditional/future sense in classical Persian.
segments.s502-g32: بسی is an adverb 'much' or 'plenty.' بر پرستش درنگ آوریم is a causative/resultative expression 'we bring/produce delay upon worship' — idiomatically 'we shall have much reason/time to linger in worship.' Note that آوریم again is first‑person plural subjunctive/optative used to express the consequent of the condition.
segments.s502-g33: نمیرد is a negative present/general‑truth verb (literally 'does not die'). کسی here with the negated verb yields the sense 'no one' (poetic indefinite). کو is an archaic relative particle (= که) introducing the relative clause 'who...'. روان is the noun 'soul/spirit' and پرورد is a compressed/poetic form related to پروردن/پرورنده (nurture / nurturer) forming the predicate of the relative clause ('whose soul is nurtured' or 'who nurtures his soul' depending on parsing). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: verb + subject + relative phrase.
segments.s502-g34: به یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'in/unto God' placed before the verb; پناهد is the (archaic/poetic) 3rd‑person singular verb 'takes refuge/puts his trust'. ز راه خرد is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by/from the way of خرد (wisdom/reason)'; ز + noun in poetry often means 'by the means of' or 'through'. The whole segment completes the relative clause describing the person who does not die.
segments.s502-g35: دو is the numeral 'two' modifying مهرست. مهرست is a poetic compound (مهر + است) meaning 'there are two مهر(s) with me' — مهر can mean charm, amulet, affection or companion depending on context; با من 'with me' follows the noun. که introduces a relative/simile clause; چون آفتاب sets up the first simile 'like the sun.'.
segments.s502-g36: بتابد is a 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive 'shines' describing the simile begun by چون آفتاب. شب تیره ('dark night') is a noun + adjective in inverted (poetic) order. چون introduces the second simile 'like', and بیند is 3rd‑person singular 'sees'; آب is the object 'water'. Together the clause contrasts two images (sun shining; water encountering dark night) that characterize the two مهر(s).
segments.s502-g37: یکی 'one' is the object being selected. زان (از آن) is the partitive/prepositional 'of/from that' pointing back to the two charms; تو 'you' is the implied beneficiary. بر گیر is an imperative 'take up/take', and در پیش باش is an imperative phrase 'be at the front / stand in front' (در پیش = ahead, in front). Imperative mood and vocative/direct address to 'تو' are used.
segments.s502-g38: نگهبان is a predicate noun 'guardian' and باش is the imperative 'be'. جان و تن خویش 'your own soul and body' uses جان (soul/life) and تن (body); خویش is the possessive 'own' formed here in two poetic parts (خوی + ش). The whole is an instruction: 'Be guardian of your soul and body.'
segments.s502-g39: دگر 'another' modifies مهره ('bead/amulet' or figuratively 'charm/ally'). باشد is a 3rd‑person present/subjunctive/copular verb here meaning 'will be / shall be' or 'is to be'. مرا is the object 'for me' (in poetry split as م + را), and شمع راه 'candle/lamp of the road' is a metaphorical apposition describing that مهره as a guide. Note the poetic splitting of مرا into م + را.
segments.s502-g40: به تاریک اندر شوم is 'into the dark I will go/enter' — به + تاریک = 'into the darkness', اندر شوم is first‑person 'I may become/enter' (شوم). با سپاه 'with an army' attaches after the verb as a circumstantial adverbial. The whole clause states the speaker's movement into darkness accompanied by troops; شوم is subjunctive/poetic future sense.
segments.s502-g9: که introduces this subordinate clause (here: 'that' / linking clause). دهقان is the subject 'the peasant'. ورا is the old-fashioned clitic phrase 'to him/it' (ورا = بر + او in older usage) functioning as an indirect object. نام ... نهاد is a verbal phrase meaning 'placed a name' — نهاد is the past tense of نهادن ('put/placed') and appears at the end, so the order is Subject (دهقان) + indirect object (ورا) + object (نام حیوان) + verb (نهاد). Here حیوان is used as the lexical string given as the name (a proper name derived from the noun).
segments.s502-g10: چو begins a temporal clause meaning 'when'. از بخشش پهلوان is a genitive/ablative phrase 'from/of the champion's gift' (بخشش = gift/donation). کردن + یاد (کرد یاد) is a light-verb construction: یاد is a noun 'remembrance' and کرد (past of کردن) turns it into the verb 'remembered' (literally 'made remembrance'). Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal marker + genitive phrase + light-verb construction.
segments.s502-g11: همی بود is an imperfective/past progressive idiom 'he kept being / he remained'. تا introduces the temporal boundary 'until'. گشت is past of گشتن 'became/turned', followed by خورشید (subject) and the predicate adjective زرد; the verb-before-subject order is a poetic inversion but reads 'until the sun became yellow'.
segments.s502-g12: فرو شد is a verb phrase 'fell/sank down' formed from the adverbial prefix فرو ('down') plus شد (past of شدن) and means 'sank'. بران = بر آن ('upon that') is an archaic/poetic prepositional form placed before the noun. چشمه لاژورد is the noun phrase 'the lapis‑blue spring' (لاژورد postmodifies چشمه in poetic order).
segments.s502-g13: ز یزدان پاک is an ablative/agent phrase 'from (the) pure God' (ز = from, poetic). آن شگفتی is the direct object 'that marvel/wonder', and بدید is the archaic past form of دیدن 'he saw'. The phrase order places the source ('ز یزدان پاک') before the object for emphasis.
segments.s502-g14: که introduces the content of the marvel ('that ...'). خورشید گشت is 'the sun became' (گشت = became), and از جهان ناپدید places the result 'vanished from the world' with ناپدید (an adjective 'vanished/invisible'). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect but reads 'that the sun became vanished from the world'.
segments.s502-g15: بیامد is past 'he came' used here as 'he returned'. به لشکرگه خویش is the prepositional phrase 'to his camp' where خویش is the possessive 'his own' (here shown as خوی + ش in the tokenization). باز is a postpositive adverb 'again'. The order is Verb + prepositional destination + adverb.
segments.s502-g16: دلی پر ز اندیشه‌های دراز is a nominal phrase 'a heart full of long thoughts': دلی (a heart/heartful), پر ز = 'full of' (پر followed by the preposition ز = 'of/from'), and اندیشه‌های دراز = 'long thoughts' with دراز postmodifying اندیشه‌ها. The whole phrase functions as an adjunct describing his state on returning.

sentences.s502-s29: He ate, rested, and returned quickly; praise kept increasing upon him.
sentences.s502-s9: The memory of the sovereign darkened the night; then he set his thought on the water of life.
sentences.s502-s10: Patient, from the army he selected first whoever he saw.
sentences.s502-s11: After fasting for forty additional days he took food; he came to the outskirts to see what marvel he might behold.
sentences.s502-s12: He placed the army in that town; one nimble fellow advanced to the fore and set himself on his feet.
sentences.s502-s25: He went on like this for two days and two nights; no one moved a lip to eat.
sentences.s502-s26: Three others, in the darkness at the fork in the road, appeared and then vanished from before Khizr the king.
sentences.s502-s27: The prophet drove the animal toward the water; he drew the head of (life) toward Kayvān (Saturn).
sentences.s502-s28: Thereupon he washed his head and body in the clear water; he sought no guardian except the Pure God.
sentences.s502-s1: From that place he led the joyful army and summoned the great, wakeful‑hearted men.
sentences.s502-s2: He went on until he came to a city whose middle and borders he could not see.
sentences.s502-s3: Everything that belonged to it lay in the wide area, full of gardens, fields, porticoes and palaces.
sentences.s502-s4: He descended, and at early dawn he came near that spring without an army.
sentences.s502-s21: Let us see what the Creator of the world has kept hidden by making this so manifest.
sentences.s502-s22: In the vanguard he is my refuge; he is the representative of my counsel and my way.
sentences.s502-s23: When the army passed toward the water (Hayvān), a cry rose — “Allahu Akbar” — from the plain.
sentences.s502-s24: When Khizr left a dwelling, he left the dishes of every kind behind.
sentences.s502-s13: He placed him in that council, (as) the chief of the famed men of that assembly.
sentences.s502-s14: Alexander came at his command; heart and life entrusted to his covenant.
sentences.s502-s15: He said to him, 'O brave, wakeful‑hearted man, set one swift detachment to this task, O heart.'
sentences.s502-s16: If we can seize the living water, we shall have much cause to linger in worship.
sentences.s502-s17: No one dies whose soul is nurtured and who takes refuge in God by the way of wisdom.
sentences.s502-s18: Two charms (or companions) are with me: one like the sun when it shines, the other like water when it meets the dark night.
sentences.s502-s19: Take one of those for yourself and be at the front; be the keeper of your own soul and body.
sentences.s502-s20: Another charm (bead) will be mine — a lamp for the road; into the dark I shall go with an army.
sentences.s502-s5: The peasant gave him the name Hayvān when he remembered the champion's generosity.
sentences.s502-s6: He remained until the sun turned yellow and sank down into that lapis‑blue spring.
sentences.s502-s7: From the pure God he saw that marvel: that the sun had vanished from the world.
sentences.s502-s8: He returned to his own camp again, his heart full of long thoughts.
